answer:
**Song and singing as social practices**—such as communal chanting, mourning laments, war songs, work songs, or bardic recitation—appear in a few works, primarily in narrative contexts depicting group activities rather than abstract discussions. The corpus lacks explicit treatments of bards reciting (beyond one archetypal example) or modern-style anthems, and many poetic references (e.g., in Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara*) are metaphorical nightingale songs rather than social depictions. Prohibitions on singing appear in Dharmaśāstras like *Manusmṛti* or *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, but these do not describe practices.[^1]

Representative examples, one per work:

- **Cynewulf, *Andreas*** (Ch. 1): A community in crisis weaves and chants a "grieving-song" and "death-song" amid catastrophe, mourning collectively as flames and floods engulf their city.[^2] > There was easily found within the city a grieving-song woven. Many a fearful one mourned his misery, they chanted a death-song.[^3]

- **Old English Elegies** ("Widsith," Ch. 14): The bard (*scop*) Widsith recites his wide travels and heroic tales before a lordly audience, a classic social role of the traveling poet preserving history orally.[^4]

- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: Vol. II*** (Ch. 16): Among North American copper-skinned peoples, a warrior inviting youth to war goes village-to-village; recruits follow to his hut *singing* a proclamation of vengeance, promising to kill, burn, enslave, and eat enemies—a communal war song mobilizing the group.[^5] > [They go] to his hut, singing: *I am going to war, I am going to avenge the death of my kinsman; I will kill, I will burn, I will bring back slaves, I will eat men.*[^6]

- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: Vol. III*** (Ch. 34): Porters and laborers in ports unload heavy loads *always singing and responding in verses* to a leader's commands, synchronizing movements in cadence to double their strength—a work song as social coordination.[^7] > [A]lways singing and responding in verses to one of them who commands, they execute all movements in cadence and double their strength by uniting them through measure.[^8]

No other works depict these as sustained social practices; Iqbal's *Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet* (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, Ch. 26) is itself a proposed children's anthem evoking unity ("Nanak's song of Oneness"), but not shown in communal use.[^9]

[^1]: See e.g. *Manusmṛti* 2.178 (prohibiting dancing/singing); *Viṣṇu Smṛti* 71.69–70.[^2]: *Andreas*, Ch. 1, ¶ p-e5ccfa.[^3]: *Andreas*, [Ch. 1/translation/#p-e5ccfa](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-e5ccfa).[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 14.[^5]: *Traité de Législation: Vol. II*, Ch. 16.[^6]: *Traité de Législation: Vol. II*, [Ch. 16/translation/#p-3588b3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/16-des-relations-qui-existent-entre-les-diverses-peup/translation/#p-3588b3).[^7]: *Traité de Législation: Vol. III*, Ch. 34.[^8]: *Traité de Législation: Vol. III*, [Ch. 34/translation/#p-52a1af](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/34-parallele-entre-la-portion-de-richesses-qui-est-la/translation/#p-52a1af).[^9]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 26, [¶ p-da6786](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).
